headline: Fertilizer spill in Henry County reaches the Maumee — up to 20,000 fish killed along 11-mile stretch; Toledo riverfront downstream
paragraphs: A liquid fertilizer spill that began in Henry County on July 3 has reached the Maumee River, triggering a fish kill in the Napoleon area and a HazMat response as of July 12. The Ohio EPA received a report July 3 that Huston Creek in Flatrock Township showed discolored water with an unusual odor; investigators determined that 28% UAN liquid fertilizer — containing ammonium nitrates — had released from the Farmers Elevator Associates property operated by Triway Nitrogen. A containment dam was placed in Huston Creek to limit the spill, but overnight rainfall on July 11–12 caused the dam to fail, allowing fertilizer-laden water to enter the Maumee. The Ohio EPA issued two violations to the responsible parties on July 11.

By July 12, a large fish kill was confirmed in the Maumee River from the mouth of Huston Creek to the area of Campbell's Soup — both locations in the Napoleon vicinity in Henry County, roughly 40 miles upstream of Toledo's downtown riverfront. The Napoleon Fire Department responded to reports of dead fish near Meyerholtz Park. Emergency sampling of the Maumee found all ammonia and nitrate levels well below regulatory thresholds of concern; the City of Napoleon is drawing water from the Wauseon raw water line as a precaution. Regional officials confirmed no immediate concern for Napoleon's drinking water supply and no restriction on recreational use of the Maumee River as of this brief.

By July 13, The Blade and 13abc confirmed the full scale of the kill: between 18,000 and 20,000 fish are estimated dead along 11 miles of the Maumee River, with fish sizes ranging from 3-inch sunfish to 40-inch carp. The Ohio EPA's on-the-ground assessment places the kill zone running from the mouth of Huston Creek through the Napoleon reach of the river. City of Napoleon officials confirmed water safety is maintained — the city continues drawing from the Wauseon raw water line — and no restriction on recreational use of the Maumee has been announced as of this brief. The responsible party (Triway Nitrogen / Farmers Elevator Associates) had hired an environmental contractor to install check dams in Huston Creek, but overnight rainfall caused those dams to fail, releasing the fertilizer-laden water. The two Ohio EPA violations issued July 11 remain the enforcement posture as of this update.
why_it_matters: The Maumee River is the backbone of Toledo's entire riverfront investment thesis — the Glass City Riverwalk, Metroparks Toledo, Glass City Metropark, and the outdoor-events calendar that underpins downtown activation all depend on a river residents trust for recreation and that presents well environmentally. A confirmed fish kill upstream, even one declared water-quality-safe by OEPA, is a reputational variable the riverfront economy cannot absorb in volume. The affected reach is near Napoleon — not Toledo's downtown riverfront — but the Maumee flows continuously downstream, and monitoring whether contamination migrates toward the Promenade Park and Glass City Metropark stretch is the key watch item. This event also surfaces the broader Maumee watershed nutrient and agricultural-runoff challenge that environmental advocates and outdoor-recreation stakeholders have long cited as the systemic risk to Toledo's water-quality-dependent development thesis.
